sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Creating dynamic, view-consistent videos of customized subjects is highly sought after for a wide range of emerging applications, including immersive VR/AR, virtual production, and next-generation e-commerce. However, despite rapid progress in subject-driven video generation, existing methods predominantly treat subjects as 2D entities, focusing on transferring identity through single-view visual features or textual prompts. Because real-world subjects are inherently 3D, applying these 2D-centric approaches to 3D object customization reveals a fundamental limitation: they lack the comprehensive spatial priors necessary to reconstruct the 3D geometry. Consequently, when synthesizing novel views, they must rely on generating plausible but arbitrary details for unseen regions, rather than preserving the true 3D identity. Achieving genuine 3D-aware customization remains challenging due to the scarcity of multi-view video datasets. While one might attempt to fine-tune models on limited video sequences, this often leads to temporal overfitting. To resolve these issues, we introduce a novel framework for 3D-aware video customization, comprising 3DreamBooth and 3Dapter. 3DreamBooth decouples spatial geometry from temporal motion through a 1-frame optimization paradigm. By restricting updates to spatial representations, it effectively bakes a robust 3D prior into the model without the need for exhaustive video-based training. To enhance fine-grained textures and accelerate convergence, we incorporate 3Dapter, a visual conditioning module. Following single-view pre-training, 3Dapter undergoes multi-view joint optimization with the main generation branch via an asymmetrical conditioning strategy. This design allows the module to act as a dynamic selective router, querying view-specific geometric hints from a minimal reference set. Project page: https://ko-lani.github.io/3DreamBooth/

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 9 1 ] . [ 1 4 2 5 8 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model Hyun-kyu Ko1, Jihyeon Park2, Younghyun Kim1, Dongheok Park2, and Eunbyung Park1 1Yonsei University 2Sungkyunkwan University https://ko-lani.github.io/3DreamBooth Fig. 1: 3D-aware video customization using our proposed framework. Given few multi-view reference images (left) and text prompt, our approach generates high-fidelity, view-consistent videos that seamlessly integrate customized 3D subjects into dynamic environments. Abstract. Creating dynamic, view-consistent videos of customized subjects is highly sought after for wide range of emerging applications, including immersive VR/AR, virtual production, and next-generation ecommerce. However, despite rapid progress in subject-driven video generation, existing methods predominantly treat subjects as 2D entities, focusing on transferring identity through single-view visual features or Equal contribution Corresponding author 2 Ko et al. textual prompts. Because real-world subjects are inherently 3D, applying these 2D-centric approaches to 3D object customization reveals fundamental limitation: they lack the comprehensive spatial priors necessary to reconstruct the 3D geometry. Consequently, when synthesizing novel views, they must rely on generating plausible but arbitrary details for unseen regions, rather than preserving the true 3D identity. Achieving genuine 3D-aware customization remains challenging due to the scarcity of multi-view video datasets. While one might attempt to fine-tune models on limited video sequences, this often leads to temporal overfitting. To resolve these issues, we introduce novel framework for 3D-aware video customization, comprising 3DreamBooth and 3Dapter. 3DreamBooth decouples spatial geometry from temporal motion through 1frame optimization paradigm. By restricting updates to spatial representations, it effectively bakes robust 3D prior into the model without the need for exhaustive video-based training. To enhance fine-grained textures and accelerate convergence, we incorporate 3Dapter, visual conditioning module. Following single-view pre-training, 3Dapter undergoes multi-view joint optimization with the main generation branch via an asymmetrical conditioning strategy. This design allows the module to act as dynamic selective router, querying view-specific geometric hints from minimal reference set. Our framework achieves high-fidelity, 3D-conditioned video generation while maintaining computational efficiency, as demonstrated through quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Keywords: Subject-Driven Video Generation 3D-Aware Video Generation Video Diffusion Models

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Imagine product designer who wants to showcase newly designed sneaker in dynamic advertisement video, where the shoe rotates, walks on various terrains, and appears under different lighting conditions. Capturing such footage traditionally requires repeated, costly filming sessions across different environments. Ideally, one would simply capture the subject once and let generative system handle the rest, placing it convincingly into any scene, from any viewpoint, and in any motion context. Or consider game developer who needs to animate custom character across dozens of diverse scenes while maintaining strict visual consistency. Such applications demand generative system that not only understands what subject looks like, but also grasps its underlying 3D structure well enough to render it faithfully across unseen viewpoints and novel scenarios. To realize such demanding applications, the generative AI community has actively explored subject-driven customization. While various structural conditioning frameworks [26, 33, 50] have been developed to control diffusion models [30], subject-driven customization has emerged as particularly pivotal branch. Early optimization-based approaches pioneered this field by binding specific subjects identity to unique identifier [9, 20, 31]. However, these text-driven methods often struggle to capture high-frequency details due to the inherent information 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 3 bottleneck of text embeddings. To address this, visual adapters were introduced to directly inject reference images into the diffusion process, which has become widely adopted approach for preserving intricate structural and identity details in 2D generation [22, 47]. Naturally, this customization paradigm has extended to customized Textto-Video (T2V) generation, driven by the success of foundational video frameworks [5, 10]. Recent studies have attempted to personalize video models for specific subjects or motions [17, 43, 45]. However, existing video customization methods predominantly rely on single-image references [7, 52] or purely textual prompts [13, 44]. Consequently, the generated subjects are inevitably bound to rigid, 2D appearance and often fail to render consistently across drastically different and unseen viewpoints. This limitation reveals lack of genuine understanding regarding the subjects underlying 3D geometry. While 3D-aware spatial generation has been actively explored in the image domain [25, 34], the explicit injection of multi-view images of 3D subject directly into video diffusion models to achieve robust 3D-consistent customization remains largely unexplored frontier. In this work, we present novel framework for 3D-aware customized video generation, unifying optimization-based personalization and adapter-based conditioning. We first introduce 3DreamBooth, which fine-tunes the generative backbone via LoRA [12] to internalize subjects 3D identity. Naïvely training on full video sequences often entangles spatial identity with temporal dynamics, causing the network to overfit to specific motion patterns. To avoid this, 3DreamBooth adopts 1-frame training paradigm [13,44]. By restricting inputs to single frames, temporal attention pathways are naturally bypassed, confining learning to spatial attributes while preserving the models pre-trained motion priors. While 3DreamBooth successfully implants the 3D identity, relying solely on single identifier often leads to inefficient optimization and the loss of finegrained textures. To address this, we introduce 3Dapter, multi-view conditioning module integrated via dual-branch architecture [37, 51]. By utilizing LoRA, 3Dapter injects multi-view spatial features while preserving foundational weights. Following single-view pre-training for robust feature extraction [37], 3DreamBooth and 3Dapter are jointly fine-tuned on multi-view images. During this phase, the main branch reconstructs target view while 3Dapter provides conditioning from minimal set of reference views. This synergy enables highly detailed, 3D-conditioned generation while maintaining significant computational efficiency. In summary, our main contributions are as follows: We address the problem of 3D-aware video customization by introducing multi-view conditioning framework that mitigates the entanglement of spatial identity and temporal dynamics in video diffusion models. We propose 3DreamBooth, 1-frame optimization strategy that integrates subject-specific 3D identity into the model without requiring multi-view video datasets, effectively leveraging the inherent 3D awareness of modern video models. Ko et al. We introduce 3Dapter , multi-view conditioning module trained through two-stage pipeline, enabling efficient convergence and precise 3D-conditioned video generation. We introduce 3D-CustomBench, curated evaluation suite for 3D-consistent video customization. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our framework outperforms existing single-reference baselines and ablation variants in generating 3D-aware and identity-preserving videos.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: 2.1 3D-Conditioned Image and Video Generation While single-view image conditioned diffusion models [37, 47, 50] have demonstrated remarkable success, conditioning diffusion models on 3D assets or multiview images remains largely unexplored. Recently, RefAny3D [14] fine-tuned FLUX [21] on curated, pose-aligned object dataset to achieve 3D-asset conditioned image generation. Although promising, extending this approach to video generation remains challenging, as acquiring large-scale pose-aligned objectvideo pair datasets is non-trivial. Concurrently, MV-S2V [35] introduced multiview conditioned text-to-video framework. Unlike our proposed 3DreamBooth, MV-S2V trains the video diffusion model on large-scale synthetic video datasets with multi-view object references, resulting in significant computational costs.

sections.title: 2.2 Subject-Driven Customization
sections.content: Subject-driven customization techniques aim to adapt diffusion foundation models to the user-provided objects, enabling their natural composition into diverse scenes. Early image customization methods achieved notable success through approaches such as textual binding [31], textual inversion [9], and visual adapters [22, 47, 50]. With the rapid advancement of video diffusion models [4, 19, 40], these techniques have been naturally extended to the video domain, enabling broader range of user-controllable functionalities. Existing customization methods generally fall into two categories: (1) trainingbased zero-shot approaches [18,24,49] and (2) optimization-based approaches [13, 44]. The former learns to integrate visual features of given subject, enabling rapid generation but often sacrificing fine-grained details. The latter better preserves the subject characteristics, yet their reliance on test-time optimization leads to slow inference. Our proposed framework unifies the strength of both paradigms, achieving faster optimization convergence while more effectively preserving 3D identity of the object in the synthesized video.

sections.title: 3.1 Rethinking DreamBooth for 3D Customization
sections.content: To achieve high-fidelity 3D customization of specific subject, we build upon the foundational concept of DreamBooth [31]. In the image domain, DreamBooth 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 5 Fig. 2: Overview of the 3DreamBooth training pipeline. (Left) From multiview images, one is selected as the target, while sampled subset serves as reference conditions alongside global prompt with unique identifier . (Right) The text and noisy target latents pass through the main branch (3DB LoRA), while reference latents pass through shared 3Dapter. Their features are concatenated for Multi-view Joint Attention. This 1-frame optimization decouples spatial geometry from temporal dynamics to efficiently learn 3D prior. successfully binds unique identifier (e.g., rare token ) to specific subject by fine-tuning the model to reconstruct the subjects appearance. However, extending this concept directly to video generation models requires deeper understanding of the interplay between spatial representation and temporal dynamics. Isolating Identity from Temporal Dynamics. Typically, video diffusion models are trained on large-scale datasets to learn both appearance and motion. However, when the objective is to inject the identity of specific subject, utilizing full video sequences of the subject for training is computationally redundant and highly prone to temporal overfitting (e.g., the model memorizing specific motion trajectory). Based on the insight that object identity is largely spatial attribute, we propose 1-frame video training paradigm. Modern video Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) [28] often process inputs via joint spatio-temporal attention. While recent video customization methods typically require explicitly freezing temporal modules [13] or inserting separate spatial adapters to learn subject identity from static images [44, 52], our approach leverages an inherent architectural property: when the input is restricted to single frame (T = 1), the temporal attention mechanism is naturally bypassed. This effectively localizes all gradient updates exclusively to spatial representations without requiring explicit architectural modifications. Consequently, we uniquely harness this mechanism to implant the subjects comprehensive 3D visual identity into the model while implicitly preserving its pre-trained temporal priors. During inference, these untouched temporal mechanics naturally extract and drive the temporal flow of the learned identity, enabling smooth, view-consistent video generation. 6 Ko et al. Eliciting the Implicit 3D Prior. The core intuition behind 3DreamBooth stems from the inherent capabilities of pre-trained video diffusion models [3, 19, 40, 46]. These models already possess robust, implicit 3D priors [6, 39]. For instance, when prompted to generate video of dog, the model naturally produces temporally coherent frames that preserve the 3D geometric consistency of the dog across different viewpoints. We hypothesize that this inherent 3D prior can be explicitly leveraged for customization. Akin to sculptor meticulously shaping piece of pottery from multiple angles, we train the model using diverse static views of the target subject. Through this multi-view DreamBooth training process, the unique identifier token gradually absorbs the geometric structures and view-dependent appearances of the object. Consequently, the token evolves beyond simple semantic identifier; it becomes consolidated 3D prior of the specific subject. During inference, querying the model with this enriched token, combined with the pre-trained temporal dynamics, successfully yields temporally consistent videos that showcase the customized object seamlessly from arbitrary viewpoints. 3. 3DreamBooth Building upon the aforementioned insights, we introduce 3DreamBooth, novel optimization paradigm designed to inject the high-fidelity 3D identity of specific subject into video diffusion models. Given set of static multi-view images of subject, denoted as = {s(i)}Ns i=1, where Ns is the number of subject views, we treat each image as single-frame video (T = 1). Each view image s(i) is paired with an universal text prompt containing the unique identifier and broad class noun (e.g., video of C). By using consistent prompt across all views, we force the model to internalize the multi-view spatial variations directly into the identifier token , rather than relying on explicit textual view descriptions. We optimize the pre-trained video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) vθ using LowRank Adaptation (LoRA). We inject trainable weights ϕ3DB into the transformer blocks (e.g., attention and MLP modules) while keeping the original model parameters, θ, frozen. Since the input is restricted to = 1, the joint spatiotemporal attention inherently operates only across the spatial tokens. This naturally focuses the parameter updates on spatial features without disrupting the pre-trained temporal dynamics. The training objective is defined by the velocity prediction loss: Ei,ϵ,t (cid:104) vθ,ϕ3DB(z(i) , t, p)2 2 (cid:105) , argmin ϕ3DB (1) where {1, . . . , Ns} is sampled view index, z(i) is the noisy latent of samt pled view s(i) at diffusion timestep t, represents the target velocity vector, and is the text prompt for the target subject. By minimizing this objective, the LoRA weights ϕ3DB learn the multi-view geometric variations of the subjects appearance. Consequently, this multi-view supervision successfully bakes the comprehensive 3D identity into the token and the network weights ϕ3DB. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 7 Fig. 3: Convergence Analysis and Detail Preservation. (A) Reconstruction Loss: Integrating 3Dapter (blue) drastically accelerates convergence compared to the 3DreamBooth baseline (gray). (B) Qualitative Comparison: 3DreamBooth alone (purple dot) struggles with high-frequency details due to the information bottleneck. In contrast, 3Dapter +3DreamBooth (yellow dot) perfectly preserves intricate textures (e.g., RIO typography) much earlier, demonstrating the efficacy of explicit visual priors. The Bottleneck of Text-Driven Customization. While the aforementioned 3DreamBooth optimization successfully binds 3D prior to the identifier token , this text-driven approach presents two critical limitations. First, the optimization process is inherently slow and computationally demanding. This inefficiency arises because the model is forced to map randomly initialized token to complex 3D visual manifold from scratch, relying solely on text conditioning without any explicit visual hints. Second, and more importantly, utilizing single token as the primary condition leads to significant loss of fine-grained details. Although LoRA layers are optimized to memorize the subject, the text embedding is inherently designed to capture coarse semantic concepts. Consequently, the token suffers from severe information bottleneck; it struggles to encode high-frequency details such as intricate textures, specific texts, or complex geometric nuances of the target subject. 3. 3Dapter To overcome these limitations, recent advancements in 2D image personalization have shifted towards visual adapters [11, 22, 26, 41, 47], which directly inject reference images into the diffusion process to preserve both identity and intricate details. Inspired by this paradigm, we propose 3Dapter, multi-view conditioning module that directly injects the target subjects spatial features into the generation process. Single-view Pre-training Recent advancements in controllable DiTs, such as OminiControl [37] and EasyControl [51], have demonstrated the efficacy of 8 Ko et al. Fig. 4: Detailed architecture of our two-stage conditioning mechanism. (A) Single-view Pre-training: The visual adapter (3Dapter ) is pre-trained using single-view references and fused via Single-view Joint Attention. (B) Multi-view Joint Optimization: trainable 3DreamBooth LoRA is added to the main branch. minimal set of multi-view reference images is processed in parallel by the shared 3Dapter. The Multiview Joint Attention acts as dynamic selective router, querying relevant view-specific geometric hints to reconstruct the target view. parameter-efficient visual adapters. These frameworks typically process condition image through dedicated LoRA branch, concatenate the resulting condition tokens with the main generation tokens, and perform joint spatio-temporal attention. Inspired by this paradigm, 3Dapter adopts dual-branch forward pass for single-view conditioning, as illustrated in Fig. 4-(A). During training, we utilize large-scale dataset of reference-target image pairs with the corresponding text prompts, {(x(i), y(i), p(i))}ND i=1, where clean reference image x(i) depicts single object on white background, whereas the target image y(i) shows the same object in diverse contextual scenes, accurately described by the accompanying text prompt p(i). Then, we optimize the 3Dapter (LoRA) weights ϕ3Dapter (while keeping the pre-trained parameters θ frozen) using the standard diffusion objective as follows: = Ei,ϵ,t (cid:104) vθ,ϕ3Dapter (z(i) , t, x(i), p(i))2 (cid:105) , (2) argmin ϕ3Dapter where {1, . . . , ND} denotes sampled dataset index, x(i) is reference image, z(i) is the noisy latent of the target image y(i) at diffusion timestep t, and p(i) represents the corresponding text prompt. To model the interaction between references, targets, and text prompts, we concatenate three tensors along the sequence dimension and leverage the spatiotemporal attention module in the original video model as follows: = [Qz, Qx, Qp], = [Ky, Kx, Kp], = [Vy, Vx, Vp], (3) where [, , ] denotes concatenation, and Qz, Qx, Qp are the Query tensors from the target, reference, and the text prompt, respectively (to avoid notational 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 9 clutter, we omit the layer indices of the network). Q, K, R(2Nimg+Ntxt)d are the concatenated Query, Key, and Value tensors, and Nimg and Ntxt denote the token sequence lengths of the images and the prompt text. Then, we perform the standard scaled dot-product attention using those concatenated tensors (Q, K, ). The reference tensors (Qx, Kx, Vx) are produced using the respective frozen weights augmented by our trainable 3Dapter (LoRA) weights, while other tensors are generated by the frozen weights from the original video model. Multi-View Conditioning via Joint Optimization After single-view pretraining, we transition to the final stage, where we jointly optimize the parameters of 3DreamBooth and 3Dapter for specific subject adaptation. Let = {s(i)}Ns i=1 denote the set of multi-view images of the subject. From this set, slightly abusing notation, we construct subset of conditioning views = {x(i)}Nc i=1 to serve as inputs to 3Dapter, such that and = Nc. In addition, we preprocess the conditioning views by masking out the image backgrounds and select conditioning views that can cover the full 360 around the subject to ensure complete spatial coverage. Given the multi-view images of the subject S, conditioning views , and an universal text prompt p, we simultaneously optimize the shared 3Dapter weights (ϕ3Dapter) and the 3DreamBooth weights (ϕ3DB) with the following objective, argmin ϕ3DB,ϕ3Dapter (cid:104) Ei,ϵ,t vθ,ϕ3DB,ϕ3Dapter (z(i) , t, , p)2 2 (cid:105) , (4) where {1, . . . , Ns} is sampled view index, and z(i) sampled view s(i) at diffusion timestep t. is the noisy latent of For each joint attention module, we produce Query, Key, and Value tensors for the subject views, conditioning views, and the text prompt. For the conditioning views, we process all conditioning images through single, shared 3Dapter rather than using separate adapters for each view. This shared architecture ensures that the network extracts consistent geometric features across different viewpoints without linearly increasing the parameter count. For the subject views and the text prompt, we use the 3DreamBooth LoRA weights ϕ3DB, and all projected tensors are concatenated, as shown in Fig. 4-(B). For example, joint Query tensor, R((1+Nc)Nimg+Ntxt)d can be written as follows: = [Qz, Q(1) , . . . , Q(Nc) , Qp], (5) where [] denotes the concatenation operation along the sequence dimension (we also omit network layer indices for brevity). When applying 3D Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE) [36] to the concatenated tensors, we assign distinct, sequential temporal indices (e.g., = 1, . . . , Nc) to each conditioning view. This explicit temporal separation prevents the spatial features across different viewpoints from entangling, ensuring that the network processes each condition separately. Because the 3Dapter already provides strong textural and high-frequency priors of the subject, the overall optimization burden is significantly alleviated. 10 Ko et al. Fig. 5: Visualization of the Dynamic Selective Router mechanism. (Left) Generated frames and four multi-view conditions provided to 3Dapter. The generated poses align with View 2 (red box). (Right) Cross-attention heatmaps across diffusion timesteps (t = 0, 20, 40). The network selectively assigns higher attention weights to the relevant view (View 2) to extract specific geometric features, rather than uniformly aggregating all conditions. Rather than redundantly memorizing every intricate visual detail from scratch, the model can focus more efficiently on learning how the subjects geometry transforms across different viewpoints. This synergy results in accelerated convergence and the preservation of high-fidelity detail, effectively overcoming the inherent limitations of standard DreamBooth. We found that this design induces an interesting emergent behavior, as shown in Fig. 5). The joint attention mechanism naturally learns to act as dynamic, selective router. Rather than uniformly aggregating all visual features, the network explicitly learns to query and extract only the relevant, view-specific geometric hints from the concatenated multi-view reference conditions to reconstruct the current target view. By actively filtering out conflicting visual signals from irrelevant views, the network receives clean, unambiguous condition information.

sections.title: 4.1 Evaluation Benchmark: 3D-CustomBench
sections.content: As 3D-aware video customization is an emerging task, there currently exists no standardized evaluation dataset. To address this, we introduce 3D-CustomBench, novel benchmark suite designed to assess multi-view consistent video generation of customized subjects. Data Collection and Criteria The benchmark consists of 30 distinct objects selected to feature complex 3D structures, non-trivial topologies, high texture resolution, and consistent lighting. While the majority of objects are sourced from the MVImgNet [48] dataset, we observed that its sequences often lack full 360 orbital coverage. Therefore, we supplement the benchmark with our custom-captured 3D objects to cover complete 360 trajectories. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 11 Optimization and Conditioning Strategy For each object, we utilize its full multi-view sequence (approximately 30 images) for 3DreamBooth optimization. We sample Nc = 4 conditioning views for 3Dapter, selected to maximize angular coverage and minimize visual overlap. Validation Prompts We utilize GPT-4o [16] to automatically generate one challenging validation prompt per object, featuring diverse backgrounds and complex dynamics (e.g., human-object interactions or environmental physics).

sections.title: 4.2 Evaluation Setup and Metrics
sections.content: To comprehensively assess our framework, we conduct extensive quantitative and qualitative comparisons against state-of-the-art subject-driven video generation baselines, VACE [18] and Phantom [24]. Multi-View Subject Fidelity We evaluate multi-view consistency using CLIPI, DINO-I, and an LLM-as-a-Judge [53] via GPT-4o [16]. After generating 360degree rotating videos and isolating the foregrounds with birefnet [54], we compute the bi-directional maximum cosine similarity between generated frames and the four condition views using CLIP [29] and DINOv2 [27]. For human-aligned evaluation, GPT-4o assesses identity preservation across Shape, Color, Detail, and Overall Identity on 15 Likert scale, averaged over five trials. Detailed protocols, mathematical definitions, and prompts are provided in the Appendix. 3D Geometric Fidelity To assess 3D geometric fidelity, we employ point cloud-based evaluation protocol. For each object in 3D-CustomBench, we reconstruct unified world-coordinate point clouds from both the ground-truth multiview images and the generated 360 rotation videos. Using Depth Anything 3 [23] and BiRefNet [54], we extract per-view depth maps and foreground masks to align the generated point clouds (Pgen) and the ground-truth-derived point clouds (Pgt) within shared coordinate frame. We then measure geometric consistency via Chamfer Distance [1], which averages two directed nearest-neighbor distances: Accuracy (dist(Pgen Pgt)) for shape adherence, and Completeness (dist(Pgt Pgen)) for surface coverage. The extraction and alignment methodology, alongside the protocol diagram, are further detailed in the Appendix. Video Quality and Text Alignment Using the validation prompts from 3D-CustomBench, we evaluate the intrinsic video quality using VBench [15]. Specifically, we report metrics across three dimensions: Aesthetic Quality, Imaging Quality, and Motion Smoothness. To measure how faithfully the generated videos reflect the text prompts, we compute video-text alignment score using ViCLIP [42]. Additional evaluation details are provided in the Appendix. 4.

sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.content: We build our framework upon HunyuanVideo-1.5 [19], employing LoRA [12] for both modules. 3Dapter is pre-trained for 100K iterations on Subjects200K [37], followed by joint optimization with 3DreamBooth for 400 iterations (learning rate 1 104). For fair comparison, the 3DreamBooth (only) ablation is identically optimized for 400 iterations. Full details are provided in the Appendix. 12 Ko et al. Fig. 6: Qualitative Comparisons. We compare our proposed framework against state-of-the-art video customization baselines, VACE and Phantom. Here, Ours denotes our full synergistic model (3Dapter +3DB ). While existing baselines struggle to reconstruct complex structures and fine-grained details (e.g., product tags and small accessories) from unseen viewpoints, our method faithfully maintains the subjects precise 3D geometry and intricate textures during dynamic 360 rotations. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model Table 1: Multi-View Subject Fidelity. sections.title: 5.1 Qualitative Results
sections.content: In Fig. 6, we compare our framework against baselines, VACE [18] and Phantom [24], using identical prompts except the subject identifier (our token versus descriptive text). Conditioned only on the first view, these methods lack spatial information for unseen regions, yielding inconsistent textures and geometries during rotation. Conversely, our framework synthesizes the full 360 geometry, preserving identity across all viewpoints.

sections.title: 5.2 Quantitative Results
sections.content: Multi-View Subject Fidelity. Table 1 evaluates how well the subjects identity is preserved across views. Our full model (3Dapter+3DB) achieves the best performance across most metrics, particularly in GPT-4o-based human-centric evaluations (Shape, Color, Detail, Overall). Regarding the slightly higher CLIPI score of VACE, we attribute this to CLIPs tendency to prioritize high-level semantic features over fine-grained geometric accuracy. Since VACE often generates plausible but incorrect textures for unseen views, it may achieve high semantic similarity despite failing to preserve consistent multi-view visual fidelity, which is more accurately captured by our DINO-I and GPT-4o metrics. 14 Ko et al. Table 3: Video Quality and Text Alignment. We evaluate the intrinsic video quality using VBench [15] and text-video alignment using ViCLIP Score (ViCLIPL/14) [42]. and denote Single-view and Multi-view conditioning, respectively. Method Type Aesthetic Quality Imaging Quality Motion Smoothness ViCLIP VACE [18] Phantom [24] 3Dapter 3DreamBooth 3Dapter+3DB 0.5915 0.5798 0.6283 0.5245 0. 70.84 70.58 71.65 73.34 74.33 0.9916 0.9934 0.9944 0.9928 0.9918 0.2663 0.2634 0.2048 0.2415 0. 3D Geometric Fidelity The superiority of our framework is most evident in 3D geometric consistency  (Table 2)  . We achieve Chamfer Distance (CD) of 0.0177, nearly halving the error compared to the most competitive single-view method (Phantom, 0.0338). Specifically, our significant lead in Completeness (0.0172) demonstrates that our multi-view conditioning effectively recovers the full 360 geometry, whereas single-view methods fail to infer the full 3D structure of unseen perspectives. Video Quality and Text Alignment. As shown in Table 3, our framework maintains high intrinsic video quality and text alignment, achieving comparable or superior performance against the baselines. Notably, we outperform existing methods in Imaging Quality while demonstrating highly competitive ViCLIP scores, ensuring that our robust 3D conditioning does not compromise the foundational generation capabilities.

sections.title: 5.3 Ablation Studies
sections.content: Our ablation variants (Table 1, 2) highlight the necessity of each component. Using 3Dapter alone (Single-view) provides strong aesthetic quality but lacks 3D consistency due to the absence of multi-view priors. Conversely, 3DreamBooth alone (Multi-view) ensures better geometry but struggles with fine-grained texture details and CLIP alignment. The joint optimization of 3Dapter +3DB yields the best trade-off, combining the robust 3D structure of 3DreamBooth with the high-frequency feature injection of 3Dapter.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced highly efficient framework for 3D-aware video customization. By decoupling spatial identity from temporal motion via 3DreamBooths 1-frame optimization, we embed subject-specific 3D priors without temporal overfitting. Furthermore, our multi-view conditioning module, 3Dapter, acts as dynamic selective router to explicitly extract relevant geometric features, preserving intricate textures. Extensive evaluations on our curated 3D-CustomBench demonstrate that our synergistic approach achieves state-of-the-art 3D geometric fidelity and fast convergence, paving the way for advanced applications in virtual production and advertising. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model

sections.title: A Additional Qualitative Results
sections.content: To further demonstrate the robust 3D-aware generation capabilities of our proposed framework, we provide additional qualitative results extending beyond the examples shown in the main paper. Diverse Subject Generation. In Figs. A1 and A2, we present generated videos across wide variety of object categories. For each subject, our 3Dapter + 3DreamBooth joint optimization framework effectively bakes the robust 3D spatial prior into the generation process. As result, the generated videos faithfully preserve the intricate 3D identity, high-frequency textures, and complex geometric structures of the subjects, even when seamlessly integrated into highly dynamic environmental contexts. Robustness Across Diverse Scenarios. Furthermore, Fig. A3 illustrates the versatility of our framework when single customized subject is driven by multiple, distinct text prompts. Despite the drastic variations in the background scenes and the presence of challenging human-object interactions, our model consistently maintains subject identity without any structural degradation. This confirms that our framework preserves the generative capabilities of the pretrained video model while achieving consistent subject customization. Ablation Study on 3Dapter Single-View Pre-training To validate the critical necessity of the single-view pre-training stage for the 3Dapter module, we conduct visual ablation study. fundamental question in our framework is whether the initial single image-to-video (I2V) pre-training is strictly required, or if the network can learn visual conditioning entirely from scratch during the joint optimization phase. We compare the generated videos using randomly initialized 3Dapter (without pre-training) against our fully pre-trained 3Dapter (with pre-training) at 0 and 400 iterations of 3Dapter +3DreamBooth joint optimization. As demonstrated in Fig. A4, without pre-training (yellow borders), the randomly initialized module lacks any fundamental visual conditioning capability. At 0 iterations, it produces meaningless noise. More importantly, even after 400 iterations of joint optimization, the model completely fails to converge. Forcing the network to simultaneously learn basic image-to-video mapping and complex multi-view spatial alignment from scratch causes severe optimization collapse, resulting in structural artifacts and loss of identity. 20 Ko et al. Fig. A1: Qualitative results across diverse subjects. Each row presents distinct object, demonstrating accurate 3D identity preservation in dynamic contexts. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 21 Fig. A2: Additional qualitative results across diverse subjects. Each row presents distinct object, further demonstrating the generalizability of our framework in preserving 3D identity across varied object categories and dynamic contexts. In contrast, the fully pre-trained 3Dapter (blue borders) provides robust structural foundation. At 0 iterations, the multi-view reference frames are processed as sequential signal under 3D RoPE (temporal+spatial) positional Ko et al. Fig. A3: Single-subject generation across diverse prompts. Given single subject (top-left), we generate videos using multiple prompts with varying scenarios. Our framework consistently preserves the subjects identity across complex scenes, including human-object interactions and richly detailed environments. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 23 Fig. A4: Effect of 3Dapter pre-training on joint optimization. Randomly initialized 3Dapter (yellow borders) versus pre-trained 3Dapter (blue borders) across different 3DreamBooth joint training iterations. Without pre-training, the model suffers optimization collapse even at 400 iterations. In contrast, the pre-trained 3Dapter enables accurate multi-view alignment and high-fidelity 3D customization within short optimization period. encoding, causing rotation artifacts. However, through joint optimization, the model learns to selectively attend to the most relevant multi-view cues, effectively acting as spatial router that extracts precise 3D structural information from the reference views. This enables accurate and high-fidelity 3D-aware video customization within just 400 iterations.

sections.title: C Training Dynamics and Qualitative Ablation
sections.content: To comprehensively validate the necessity and synergistic effect of our proposed modules, we conduct an extended analysis focusing on both the training dynamics and comparative qualitative ablation. 24 Ko et al. Fig. A5: Comprehensive qualitative ablation across three configurations. Comparing 3Dapter only, 3DreamBooth only (at 400 and 1600 iterations), and our full framework at 400 iterations (3Dapter +3DreamBooth). Compared to our full model (3Dapter +3DreamBooth), 3Dapter alone fails to maintain 3D geometric volume and view consistency, while 3DreamBooth alone lacks structural precision and texture fidelity even with 4 more iterations. C.1 Comprehensive Qualitative Ablation Fig. A5 presents comprehensive visual ablation study that complements the convergence analysis. We compare three configurations: 3Dapter only, 3DreamBooth only (at 400 and 1600 iterations), and our full framework 3Dapter + 3DreamBooth. When deploying 3Dapter alone, the model fails to guarantee accurate 3D spatial volume and view consistency, as zero-shot visual conditioning struggles to build robust 3D prior. Conversely, while the fully converged 3DreamBooth (1600 iterations) maintains multi-view consistency, it still falls short of capturing the exact structural precision and crisp textures. In contrast, our full framework (3Dapter +3DreamBooth), benefiting from the explicit multi-view visual hints provided by 3Dapter, seamlessly integrates precise spatial conditions with robust 3D volume. It achieves exceptional identity and detail preservation within just 400 iterations. This comprehensive comparison supports that our dynamic 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 25 Fig. A6: Step-by-step training dynamics of the 3DreamBooth-only baseline. Generated frames at 0, 400, 800, 1200, 1600, and 2000 training iterations illustrate the convergence dynamics of the 3DreamBooth-only baseline. While the coarse shape and color of the reference plushie are visible from early iterations, accurate subject identity and view-consistent geometry emerge only with prolonged optimization. selective routing mechanism not only drastically accelerates the convergence of 3D customization but is also indispensable for high-fidelity detail preservation. Ko et al. Fig. A7: Extension to DiT-based Video Models. We demonstrate the extensibility of 3DreamBooth to state-of-the-art Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures by applying our fine-tuning paradigm to the Wan 2.1 text-to-video model. Even without explicit spatial conditioning, the generated sequences faithfully preserve the intricate 3D identity and complex geometric structures of the subjects across diverse contexts. C.2 3DreamBooth Training Dynamics As discussed in the main text, relying solely on text-driven optimization (3DreamBooth) introduces an information bottleneck, leading to notably slow convergence rate. To explicitly demonstrate this, we analyze the step-by-step training dynamics of the 3DreamBooth (only) baseline. As shown in Fig. A6, we extract generated frames at 400, 800, 1200, 1600, and 2000 training iterations. At 400 iterations, while the baseline captures the coarse spatial volume of the subject, it struggles to reconstruct high-frequency details (e.g., intricate textures, logos, or typography). Although prolonged training up to 2000 iterations gradually refines the subjects appearance, fine-grained details such as textures and typography remain poorly reconstructed, indicating fundamental limitation of text-driven optimization alone. Extensibility of 3DreamBooth to Other DiT

sections.title: Architectures
sections.content: To demonstrate the generalizability and robustness of our core optimization strategy, we investigate whether the one-frame training paradigm of 3DreamBooth can be effectively applied to other state-of-the-art Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based video models. To isolate and explicitly evaluate the extensibility of this fine-tuning paradigm, we apply 3DreamBooth independently, without the explicit spatial conditioning of the 3Dapter module. We implement this on the widely adopted Wan 2.1 text-to-video (T2V) model [40]. 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 27 As illustrated in Fig. A7, even when relying solely on the 3DreamBooth optimization paradigm without external spatial priors, the framework successfully binds the complex multi-view identity to the unique identifier . The generated results demonstrate that the multi-view spatial priors are successfully injected into the identifier and accurately reflected in the synthesized videos. This confirms that our fundamental one-frame optimization approach is highly extensible and can adapt to diverse, state-of-the-art backbone architectures.

sections.title: E Discussion and Limitations
sections.content: Inefficiency of Repurposing Task-Specific Baselines. An intuitive alternative to our framework might be directly applying the 3DreamBooth multi-view fine-tuning to existing zero-shot DiT models (e.g., VACE or Phantom) without decoupled conditioning branch. However, these task-specific models entangle video generation and visual conditioning within single unified pathway. Fine-tuning the entire heavily parameterized model using multi-view reconstruction objective is not only profoundly inefficient but also unstable, as it risks corrupting the core video generation capabilities. In practice, attempting this brute-force joint training easily leads to Out-of-Memory (OOM) failures. To contextualize the computational burden, training baselines like VACE required 128 A100 GPUs, and Phantom reported utilizing over 30,000 A100 GPU hours. In contrast, our decoupled 3Dapter module was efficiently pre-trained in just 4 days using only 4 RTX Pro 6000 GPUs, proving that our modular approach is fundamentally more sustainable and efficient. Test-Time Optimization vs. Zero-Shot Generation. One might point to the requirement of test-time optimization as limitation compared to pure zeroshot methods. However, achieving true zero-shot 3D-aware video customization would require an unimaginably massive dataset of strictly aligned multi-view and video pairs. Currently, such datasets do not exist, and curating them from real-world data is prohibitively difficult. Furthermore, even if such data were available, the required training scale would be astronomical. We elegantly circumvent this bottleneck via our efficient one-frame optimization paradigm. More importantly, in practical real-world applications (e.g., the advertising industry or commercial content creation), the absolute preservation of fine-grained subject details is paramount. For such use cases, the minor trade-off of short testtime optimization is vastly outweighed by the uncompromising, high-fidelity 3D structural accuracy that our method guarantees over zero-shot approximations. Semantic Interactions and Future Work. Our qualitative results reveal compelling emergent property: through 3DreamBooth training, the unique identifier successfully inherits the full spectrum of semantic interactions natively associated with the prior class noun C. If the class noun can perform an action, the customized subject can replicate it flawlessly within the generated video. However, our current experiments primarily focus on rigid or static objects. It remains an open question how this paradigm adapts to highly dynamic subjects with complex articulations (e.g., human bodies) or objects undergoing drastic 28 Ko et al. Fig. A8: Visual comparison and convergence analysis. (Left) Ablation study on the components of our framework. Single-view baselines (VACE, Phantom) and individual modules (3Dapter, 3DreamBooth) struggle to maintain fine-grained identity details, whereas the synergistic combination of 3Dapter +3DreamBooth successfully achieves accurate identity preservation. (Right) Training loss curve and qualitative progression of 3DreamBooth across optimization steps, demonstrating that the model requires prolonged optimization process to fully converge and refine subject details. state changes over time. Exploring these temporal state variations represents an exciting direction for future research. Additionally, extending this optimization framework to video editing models (leveraging reference videos) could unlock highly robust, 3D-aware subject insertion in real-world footage, further broadening the impact of our approach.

sections.title: F Ablation on Visual Conditioning and Convergence
sections.content: To comprehensively validate the necessity of our dual-branch design, we perform visual ablation study comparing our framework against existing baselines and isolated modules. As illustrated in the left panel of Fig. A8, zero-shot video customization baselines (VACE and Phantom) struggle to preserve finegrained high-frequency details, such as the precise typography and logo on the target subject. Furthermore, as discussed in the main paper and elaborated in the subsequent qualitative sections, these baselinesalong with deploying 3Dapter alonefail to maintain accurate 3D spatial volume and view consistency. Focusing back on the single-view detail preservation in Fig. A8, relying solely on 3DreamBooth for 400 optimization steps captures the coarse geometry but produces garbled text due to the inherent information bottleneck of text embeddings. In contrast, our synergistic framework (3Dapter +3DreamBooth) successfully achieves highly accurate identity and detail preservation. Furthermore, the right panel of Fig. A8 provides detailed convergence analysis of the 3DreamBooth (only) baseline. The reconstruction loss curve and the corresponding generated frames across extended optimization steps (from 400 to 1600) reveal that relying purely on text-driven optimization requires sig3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 29 nificantly prolonged training period to refine intricate textures. Even at 1600 steps, the generated text remains less crisp compared to our full frameworks result achieved at just 400 steps. Additional qualitative video comparisons are provided in Figs. A5 and A6.

sections.title: G Implementation Details
sections.content: G.1 Base Model and LoRA Configurations We build our framework upon the state-of-the-art open-source video diffusion model, HunyuanVideo-1.5 [19]. To efficiently inject the 3D multi-view prior without disrupting the pre-trained spatio-temporal dynamics, we employ Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [12] for both the 3Dapter and the 3DreamBooth modules. Specifically, we set the LoRA rank to 16 and the alpha scaling factor to 32 across all transformer blocks (e.g., attention and MLP modules). For inference, we generate 720p videos of 81 frames with 50 denoising steps. Notably, baseline methods such as VACE [18] and Phantom [24] are built upon WAN [40] (14B parameters) and require large-scale task-specific retraining of the entire backbone. In contrast, our framework builds upon HunyuanVideo-1.5 [19] (8.3B parameters) and trains only 95.62M parameters (1.15% of the backbone) via LoRA, without any modification to the pre-trained weights. This highlights the parameter efficiency of our adaptation strategy. G.2 3Dapter Pre-training and Dataset Curation For the single-view pre-training of 3Dapter, we utilize the Subjects200K dataset [37]. Curated specifically for subject-driven generation tasks, this dataset effectively addresses the overfitting risks associated with using identical image pairs by providing natural variations in pose, lighting, and contextual backgrounds. The automated construction pipeline begins with GPT-4o [16] generating over 30,000 distinct subject descriptions across multiple scenarios. These descriptions are systematically formulated into structured prompts for the FLUX.1 model [21], which synthesizes paired images that share the exact subject identity but differ in their contextual layout. To guarantee high fidelity, GPT-4o acts as an automated evaluator to discard any misaligned samples. During the single-view pre-training phase, we leverage this identity-consistent dataset to train 3Dapter to robustly extract and inject subject-specific spatial features into the generation process using only single reference image. To achieve parameterefficient learning while effectively integrating these visual conditions, we apply Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [12] to the key image-processing modules of the architecture. Specifically, the trainable LoRA weights, configured with rank of = 16 and scaling parameter of α = 32, are injected into the initial image projection layer, all query, key, value, and output projection matrices within the attention blocks, as well as the fully-connected layers of the multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). The module is optimized for 100, 000 iterations with global Ko et al. batch size of 4 (1 per GPU) and learning rate of 1 104 using the AdamW optimizer. This pre-training phase was conducted on 4 NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPUs and took approximately 4 days. G.3 Test-Time Joint Optimization and Hardware Setup During the test-time joint optimization phase for each subject in our 3D-CustomBench, we explicitly preprocess all reference multi-view images to remove backgrounds using the birefnet-massive model [54]. This preprocessing step ensures that the visual distribution of the test-time references is consistent with the background-free condition images from the Subjects200K dataset used during the 3Dapter pre-training phase, reducing the domain gap and allowing the network to focus on the subjects 3D geometry without background interference. For the multi-view joint optimization of both the 3Dapter and 3DreamBooth modules, we maintain the same LoRA hyperparameters (r = 16, α = 32) utilized during pre-training. While 3Dapter retains its previously defined target modules, the LoRA weights for 3DreamBooth are systematically injected into both image and text processing pathways. Specifically, these target modules encompass the input projection layers, the query, key, value, and output projection matrices across both text and image attention blocks, and their corresponding MLPs. Empirically, we found that applying LoRA to the text processing branches is particularly crucial for successful personalization; we hypothesize that this enables the model to effectively bind the unique identifier to the subjects highly specific 3D geometric features within the attention space. The joint optimization process is conducted for 400 iterations with batch size of 1 and learning rate of 1 104. All subject-specific test-time optimizations were performed on single NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 GPU, requiring approximately 13 minutes per subject.

sections.title: H Detailed Evaluation Protocols
sections.content: H.1 Multi-View Subject Fidelity and LLM-as-a-Judge For the multi-view subject fidelity evaluation, we first generate standardized 360-degree rotation video for each object in our 3D-CustomBench using the following prompt template: video of centered against pure, solid white background. The camera smoothly orbits 360 degrees, keeping the entire object fully in frame, high quality where denotes the learned unique identifier token and is replaced with the corresponding class noun for each subject (e.g., plushie, mug). The plain white background is deliberately chosen to isolate the subject from environmental distractors, ensuring that all downstream metrics capture subject fidelity rather than scene-level artifacts. All videos are generated at 81 frames with 50 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model Fig. A9: Pipeline of the 3D geometric fidelity evaluation. Generated frames and ground-truth images are independently lifted to 3D point clouds using depth estimation and foreground masking. The reconstructed point clouds are then aligned to compute the Chamfer Distance. denoising steps. For baseline methods that do not employ learned identifier token (e.g., VACE [18], Phantom [24]), we replace with concise visual description of the subject generated by GPT-4o [16] (e.g., red ceramic mug with deer illustration). For feature-based multi-view consistency evaluation, we utilize the [CLS] tokens from pre-trained CLIP [29] and DINOv2 [27] models. Because the camera orbits the subject in our generated videos, valid generated frame should strongly align with at least one specific condition view. Thus, we compute the cosine similarity between each generated frame and the four condition views, select the maximum similarity score for that frame, and average these maximums across the entire video. To complement these metrics, we designed an LLM-as-a-Judge [53] protocol using GPT-4o [16]. We prompt GPT-4o with the four condition views alongside 10 uniformly sampled frames from the generated video. The reference images and video frames are provided as separate images in the multimodal message, labeled with text headers to distinguish the two groups. We use temperature of 0.1 to reduce output variance while allowing minimal stochasticity. Each dataset-method pair is evaluated 5 times independently, and we report the mean and standard deviation of the scores. The model evaluates identity preservation across four dimensions: Shape Preservation: Adherence to the original silhouette and overall shape across viewpoints. Color Preservation: Consistency of color patterns and distribution. Detail Preservation: Retention of fine textures, texts, and logos. Overall Identity: Overall recognizability of the subject. Each dimension is scored on 15 scale, where 1 indicates completely unrecognizable subject and 5 indicates nearly perfect identity preservation. The exact GPT-4o prompt used for this evaluation is provided below: 32 Ko et al. You are an expert judge evaluating how well an AI-generated video preserves the identity of reference subject/object. You are given: 1. Reference images of the original subject 2. Frames extracted from generated video Evaluate the following aspects of Subject Identity Preservation on scale of 1-5: (1) Shape Preservation - Does the generated subject maintain the same overall shape and silhouette as the reference across viewpoints? (2) Color Preservation - Are the colors, color patterns, and color distribution of the subject consistent with the reference? (3) Detail Preservation - Are fine details (textures, logos, labels, patterns, small features) preserved accurately? (4) Overall Identity - Considering all aspects holistically, would human recognize this as the same specific object/subject? Scoring guide: - 1 = Completely different, unrecognizable - 2 = Vaguely similar but major identity differences - 3 = Recognizably similar but noticeable differences in some aspects - 4 = Clearly the same subject with only minor differences - 5 = Nearly perfect identity preservation You MUST output ONLY in this exact format (no other text): shape_preservation: <score> color_preservation: <score> detail_preservation: <score> overall_identity: <score> H.2 3D Geometric Fidelity Alignment Protocol To explicitly evaluate the 3D structural accuracy, we designed depth-based point cloud alignment protocol (Fig. A9). For each object in our 3D-CustomBench, we obtain two sets of views: the ground-truth multi-view images and frames sampled from the 360 rotation videos generated in Sec. H.1. We independently process the frames using Depth Anything 3 [23] to estimate per-view metric depth maps. Concurrently, BiRefNet [54] is employed to obtain precise foreground segmentation masks. The estimated depth maps are then masked and back-projected into unified world-coordinate point clouds. Since the generated point cloud (Pgen) and the ground-truth point cloud (Pgt) reside in different arbitrary coordinate frames, we align them via threestage registration process: 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model 33 Fig. A10: Qualitative comparison of reconstructed 3D point clouds. Using the evaluation pipeline described above, we lift each methods generated 360 rotation video into colored point clouds and visualize them from four different viewpoints. The leftmost column shows the reference condition used for each method: for ours, representative subset of the 30 multi-view images is displayed, while single-view baselines (Phantom [24] and VACE [18]) rely on single reference image. Our framework produces geometrically coherent 3D structure, while Phantom [24] yields over-smoothed geometry and VACE [18] exhibits fragmented surfaces with structural artifacts. 1. Coarse Alignment: We extract FPFH [32] features and perform global registration using RANSAC [8]. 2. Fine Alignment: We apply the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) [2] algorithm for precise rigid registration. 3. Scale Correction: Finally, we utilize the Umeyama similarity transformation [38] to correct any residual scale differences between the two spaces. Following this alignment, the Chamfer Distance [1] is computed as the average of the Accuracy and Completeness metrics to evaluate the geometric fidelity. Fig. A10 visualizes representative point cloud reconstructions from each method, illustrating the geometric differences captured by this protocol. H.3 Video Quality and Text Alignment Metrics For each object in 3D-CustomBench, we use GPT-4o [16] to automatically generate one validation prompt that describes realistic, cinematic scenario featuring the subject in natural context (e.g., human-object interactions or dynamic environments). To measure how faithfully the generated video reflects the given text prompt, we compute video-text alignment score using ViCLIP [42]. For 34 Ko et al. each generated video, we uniformly sample 8 frames and feed them jointly into the ViCLIP-L/14 vision encoder. This encoder applies temporal attention across the frames to produce single, comprehensive video-level embedding. The final ViCLIP Score is calculated as the cosine similarity between this video-level embedding and the text embedding of the corresponding validation prompt extracted by the ViCLIP text encoder.
